conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm inquiring about my son Alex - he's 17 and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a few questions to help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

First, is Alex currently in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, he actually left school recently, but he's just signed up with the careers service to figure out what he wants to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know.

Can you tell me Alex's exact date of birth, or at least confirm whether he is still under 16, or if he's 16, 17, 18, or 19 years old?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. To clarify further - you mentioned Alex left school recently and has signed up with the careers service. 

Is Alex currently enrolled in any approved education or training programme (such as A-levels, vocational qualifications, or an apprenticeship), or is he registered with the careers service but not yet in education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not enrolled in any courses or training at the moment. He's just registered with the careers service while he figures out his next steps.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Just to confirm - Alex is 17, not currently in education or training, and is registered with the careers service.

Can you tell me when he turned 16, or when he left education? I need to understand if he's still within the extension period that applies after a child turns 16.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He turned 16 and left school earlier this year. It's been a few months now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. One more important question:

Does Alex live with you, and are you responsible for him? Also, are you (or your partner if you have one) currently the person claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or would this be a new claim?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me and I'm responsible for him. This would be about continuing my claim - I've been claiming Child Benefit for him already.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## My Assessment

Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid **you are not currently eligible to continue claiming Child Benefit for Alex**.

**Here's why:**

Child Benefit can be claimed for:
- Children under 16, OR
- Young people aged 16 to 19 who are in **approved education or training**

Since Alex is 17 and left school earlier this year, he falls into the second category. However, because he is not currently enrolled in any approved education or training programme, he does not meet the eligibility criteria for the 16-19 age extension.

While it's positive that Alex has registered with the careers service to explore his options, registration alone does not constitute approved education or training.

Your Child Benefit entitlement for Alex would have ended when he left approved education, or on 31st August following his 16th birthday (whichever was later).

**Good news for the future:** If Alex decides to enroll in approved education or training (such as college courses, A-levels, vocational qualifications, or an apprenticeship) and is still under 20 years old, you may be able to reclaim Child Benefit for him at that point.

If you have any questions or if Alex's circumstances change, please don't hesitate to reach out.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and not currently in approved education or training. According to UK Child Benefit rules, you can claim Child Benefit for children under 16, or for those aged 16 to 19 if they are in approved education or training. Since Alex left school earlier this year when he turned 16 and is not enrolled in any approved education or training programme, he does not meet the eligibility criteria for the 16-19 age extension. While he is registered with the careers service, this alone does not constitute approved education or training. Child Benefit entitlement would have ended when Alex left approved education or training, or on the 31st August following his 16th birthday (whichever was later). Therefore, you are not eligible to continue claiming Child Benefit for Alex at this time. If Alex enrolls in approved education or training in the future and is still under 20, you may be able to reclaim Child Benefit for him.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 17 years old and lives at home. While Alex meets the residency requirement and the claimant is responsible for him, he does not meet the age-related education criteria. Alex left school when he turned 16 earlier this year and is not currently in approved education or training. Although registered with the careers service, this does not qualify as approved education or training. Therefore, Child Benefit eligibility has ended for Alex.
